Problem Statement: Find maximum sum of a contiguous subarray (Kadane's Algorithm).

Problem Statement: Find median of two sorted arrays.

The goal is to find a split where the elements on the left of the partition are all ≤ the elements on the right of the partition in the merged array. Problem Statement: Find subarray with a given sum.

Problem Statement: Find the missing number in an arraycontaining n distinct numbers from range 1 to N

Problem Statement: Find minimum flips to make binary string monotone increasing.

Problem Statement: Find all combinations of factors for a given number.
Hints: Use Backtracking/DFS. For a number n, iterate through all possible factors i from 2 up to n​ (or the previous factor). Add i to the current combination, and recursively find factors for the quotient n/i.
Notes: Easy to come up with approach, might face issue in code implementation

Problem Statement: Generate all letter combinations from a phone number.
Hints: Use Backtracking/DFS. The depth of the recursion is the length of the digits string. At each level of the recursion, iterate through the characters corresponding to the current digit and append them to the current combination before making the recursive call.
Notes: Chose bit incorrect approach of using string manipulation without recursion

Problem Statement: Count number of paths from top-left to bottom-right in a grid.
Hints: DP State: dp[i][j] is the number of ways to reach cell (i,j). Recurrence: dp[i][j]=dp[i−1][j]+dp[i][j−1]. Alternatively, this is a simple combinatorics problem: (m−1m+n−2​) or (n−1m+n−2​) (choosing m−1 'Down' moves from a total of m+n−2 moves).
Notes: Easy to come up with the approach. Came up with iterative appraoch and added it also to solution

Problem Statement: Return all possible combinations of k numbers chosen from the range [1, n]
Hints: Build combinations one number at a time using backtracking. Always move forward (use a start pointer) to avoid duplicates, and prune early when remaining numbers can't complete the target size

Problem Statement: Find all unique combinations that sum to a target. (Each number can be used multiple time)
Hints: Use Backtracking/DFS. The key is that elements can be reused. In the recursive call, if an element candidates[i] is used, the next call can still use candidates[i] (by passing i as the starting index).
Notes: Can come up with approach and corrected the solution

Problem Statement: Find all unique combinations that sum to target (each number used once).
Hints: Use Backtracking/DFS. The key is that elements are unique for each combination (i.e., cannot be reused) and there are duplicates in the input array. Sort the array and use a check: if index>start and candidates[index]=candidates[index−1], then skip the current element to avoid duplicate combinations.
Notes: Got confused on why the duplicates needs to be skipped in the skip branch of recursion

Problem Statement: Restore valid IP addresses from a string of digits.
Hints: Use Backtracking/DFS to try partitioning the string into four parts. Each part must be a valid IP segment (length 1 to 3, value ≤255, and no leading zeros unless the segment is "0"). The recursion depth is 4.

Problem Statement: Implement a calculator supporting +, -, *, /.
Hints: Use a Stack to store numbers. Process multiplication and division immediately. For addition/subtraction, simply push the number (or its negative for subtraction) onto the stack. The final result is the sum of all elements in the stack.
Notes: Got the basic approach of handling the / x before + - but faced issue in the stack type and character to number conversion

Problem Statement: Implement a calculator supporting +, -, *, /, and parentheses.
Hints: This is essentially Basic Calculator II logic nested with recursion for parentheses. Use a Stack to handle operator precedence (multiplication/division first). When an opening parenthesis '(' is encountered, make a recursive call to evaluate the sub-expression. When a closing parenthesis ')' is encountered, return the result of the current sub-expression.
Notes: Very different from basic calculator 2. Didn't think of the recursive appraoch

Problem Statement: Check how many parentheses needs to be deleted to make parentheses balanced and valid.
Patterns: Stack, String Manipulation, Multiple Parenthesis Types
Hints: Use a Stack to track unclosed open parentheses. When a closing parenthesis is encountered, check if the stack top is the matching open parenthesis. The added complexity often involves matching two types of parentheses or a mix of stars and parentheses.
Notes: Didn't understood question properly and faced issue in the approach

Problem Statement: Implement an LRU (Least Recently Used) cache.
Patterns: Data Structure Design, Doubly Linked List, Hash Map
Hints: The O(1) complexity requires a data structure that allows O(1) look-up and O(1) deletion/insertion. Combine a HashMap (to store key → node address) with a Doubly Linked List (to store the usage order). The least recently used (LRU) item is always at the tail of the list.
Notes: Tricky approach and difficult implementation

Problem Statement: Find starting gas station to complete circular route.
Hints: The key is that if the total gas is ≥ total cost, a solution must exist. Start at index 0. Track a ‘current_balance‘. If ‘current_balance‘ becomes negative, reset the ‘current_balance‘ to 0 and set the start position to the next index.
Notes: Unintuitive. Got confused on why we don't need to loop the array till the start of the trip.

Problem Statement: Determine if a graph is bipartite.
Hints: A graph is bipartite if its nodes can be divided into two disjoint sets such that every edge connects a node in one set to one in the other. Use BFS or DFS to traverse the graph and color the nodes with two colors, ensuring adjacent nodes have different colors. Check all components.
Notes: Had forgot the problem and the way to approach. Need to ensure that we are checking for all the subgraphs

Problem Statement: Minimize maximum cost after dividing into k components.
Hints: This is a variation of the Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) problem. Kruskal's or Prim's algorithm finds the minimum total cost. The variation often involves finding the K largest or K smallest edges in the MST.
Notes: Forgot kurskal and Prim algo, need to revise

Problem Statement: Count n-digit numbers with increasing digits.
Implement an LRU (Least Recently Used) cache.
Patterns: Data Structure Design, Doubly Linked List, Hash Map
Hints: (See previous entry for LruCacheTest). Combine a HashMap (key → node address) with a Doubly Linked List (usage order) for O(1) look-up and O(1) update/delete.
Notes: Faced issue with the data structures to use

Problem Statement: GIven an interger array, find the sum of the minimum value of all possible contiguous subarrays.
Hints: The contribution of A[i] to the total sum is A[i]×(number of subarrays where A[i] is the minimum). Use a Monotonic Stack to find the index of the nearest smaller element to the left and the nearest smaller element to the right of A[i].
Notes: Unintuitive approach. Iterate through all the indices and find all the subarrays for which this woud be min. Then calculate the contirubtion of this index in the final result

Problem Statement: Design a stack that supports push, pop, top, and retrieving minimum in O(1).
Hints: To achieve O(1) for ‘getMin‘, use an Auxiliary Stack to track the current minimum element at each state of the main stack. When pushing, push the new element to the main stack and min(new_element,current_min) to the auxiliary stack.
Notes: Difficulty with the approach. Forgot about min Stack, where I can store the minimums. Easy implementation

Problem Statement: Find largest rectangle in histogram.
Hints: Use a Monotonic Increasing Stack to find, for each bar, the first smaller bar to the left and the first smaller bar to the right. These two indices define the maximum width for which the current bar is the height. The maximum area is max(height[i]×(right_index−left_index−1)).
Notes: Difficult to think about the solution which uses monotonic stack. Code understanding is also difficult where while loop is created and finding the boundaries

Problem Statement: Find next greater element for each element in array.
Hints: Use a Monotonic Stack (decreasing order). Iterate through the second array nums2. When pushing an element, pop all smaller elements from the stack; the element being pushed is their Next Greater Element. Store the results in a HashMap.
Notes: Easy to come up with the approach as previous questions with monotonic stack were practiced

Problem Statement: Find next greater element in circular array.
Hints: This is the same as the non-circular version, but you need to simulate a circular array. This can be achieved by iterating through the array twice (or iterating once over the concatenated array nums+nums). The stack stores indices, and the array size is handled by indexmodN.
Notes: Not intuitive approach

Problem Statement: Remove consecutive duplicates recursively.
Hints: Use a Stack (or a string/list used as a stack) to build the result. When processing the current character, check the top of the stack. If they match, pop (delete) until the duplicate sequence is cleared. If they don't match, push the current character.
Notes: Easy to come up with the approach as previous questions with monotonic stack were practiced

Problem Statement: Given tasks with [enqueueTime, processingTime], simulate single-threaded CPU task scheduling which prefer to pick tasks with shortest processing time
Hints: Use two Min-Heaps. One to store available tasks (ordered by processing time, then index) and one to store pending tasks (ordered by enqueue time). At each step, move all tasks that have arrived (enqueue time ≤ current time) from the pending heap to the available heap. Then, greedily pick the task with the minimum processing time from the available heap.

Problem Statement: Count number of nice subarrays. Nice subarray is subarray with exactly k odd numbers.
Patterns: Hash Map, Prefix Sum, Sliding Window
Hints: Replace odd numbers with 1 and even numbers with 0. The problem reduces to finding the number of subarrays with a sum equal to K. Use the Prefix Sum + HashMap technique (similar to SumSubarrayFinder).

Problem Statement: Implement a queue using stacks.
Hints: Use Two Stacks, ‘input_stack‘ and ‘output_stack‘. Push goes to the ‘input_stack‘. Pop and Peek check the ‘output_stack‘. If ‘output_stack‘ is empty, transfer all elements from ‘input_stack‘ to ‘output_stack‘ before popping/peeking.
Notes: Using single stack is hard

Problem Statement: Evaluate Reverse Polish Notation expression.
Hints: Use a Stack. Iterate through the expression. If the token is a number, push it onto the stack. If the token is an operator, pop the two operands, perform the operation, and push the result back onto the stack.

Problem Statement: Find first non-repeating character in a string.
Hints: Use a Queue to maintain the order of characters seen so far. Use a HashMap to store the frequency of each character. When a character is added, update its frequency in the map. Then, dequeue elements from the front of the queue until the element at the front has a frequency of 1 (or the queue is empty).
Notes: Difficult approach. Didn't understand the correct data structures to be used. Forgot the ds linked hashmap and tree maps etc

Problem Statement: Implement an LFU (Least Frequently Used) cache.
Patterns: Data Structure Design, Doubly Linked List, Hash Map
Hints: Use three maps: key→value, key→frequency, and frequency→keys. When a key’s frequency increases, move it to the next bucket; if the old bucket becomes empty and was the minimum, increment minFrequency. New keys always start at frequency 1, so minFrequency resets to 1.

Problem Statement: Validate parentheses in an expression.
Hints: Use a Stack. Push open parentheses onto the stack. When a closing parenthesis is encountered, check if the stack is non-empty and the top of the stack is the matching open parenthesis; if so, pop. If not, the string is invalid. The stack must be empty at the end for the string to be valid.
Notes: Easy solution and appraoch

Problem Statement: Find maximal rectangle containing only 1s in binary matrix.
Hints: This problem reduces to the Largest Rectangle in Histogram problem for each row. DP State: height[j] is the number of consecutive 1's ending at matrix[i][j] in the column j. Iterate through each row, update the ‘height‘ array, and then apply the Monotonic Stack (Largest Histogram) solution on the ‘height‘ array.

Problem Statement: Find intersection point of two linked lists.
Hints: Use Two Pointers, ptrA starting at headA and ptrB starting at headB. When a pointer reaches ‘null‘, redirect it to the head of the other list (e.g., ptrA goes to headB). After at most two full traversals, the pointers will either meet at the intersection node or both will be ‘null‘ (if there is no intersection).
Notes: Approach of solution without finding length is tricky

Problem Statement: Swap every two adjacent nodes in a linked list.
Hints: Use a Dummy Node to handle the head. Use an iterative approach with three pointers: ‘prev‘, ‘first‘, and ‘second‘. The key is updating ‘prev‘. ‘prev‘.next points to ‘second‘, ‘first‘.next points to ‘second‘.next, and ‘second‘.next points to ‘first‘. Then ‘prev‘ moves to ‘first‘.
Notes: really hard to understand the pointers movement

Problem Statement: Reverse nodes in k-group in a linked list.
Hints: Use a Dummy Node. Use a helper function or recursive logic to reverse a sublist of size K. Before reversing, check if there are at least K nodes remaining. The key is correctly connecting the tail of the reversed group to the head of the next group, and the ‘prev‘ node to the head of the reversed group.

Problem Statement: Implement basic linked list operations.
Hints: Basic operations involve traversing, inserting, and deleting nodes. Remember to handle the head and tail cases, and use a prev pointer for deletions.

Problem Statement: Rotate a linked list to the right by k places.
Hints: First, find the length L. Then, normalize k as k=kmodL. Connect the tail to the head to form a ring. The new head is at position L−k. Break the ring at the node just before the new head.

Problem Statement: Find middle element of a linked list.
Hints: Use the Slow and Fast Pointers technique. The ‘slow‘ pointer moves by 1, and the ‘fast‘ pointer moves by 2. When ‘fast‘ reaches the end, ‘slow‘ will be at the middle of the list.

Problem Statement: Reverse a singly linked list.
Hints: Use Three Pointers: ‘prev‘ (starts at null), ‘curr‘ (starts at head), and ‘next_node‘. In a loop, set ‘curr‘.next=‘prev‘, update ‘prev‘=‘curr‘, and ‘curr‘=‘next_node‘.
Notes: Faced difficulty in follow up of reverse in group

Problem Statement: Reverse a linked list from position m to n.
Hints: Use a Dummy Node. Traverse to the node before the start position (m). Mark this as ‘prev‘. Then, perform the standard iterative reversal on the sublist from m to n, keeping track of the tail (‘m_node‘). The key is reconnecting the list: ‘prev‘.next=new_head (which is the node at n), and ‘m_node‘.next=node_after_n.

Problem Statement: Check if a linked list is a palindrome.
Hints: 1) Find the middle of the list using Slow/Fast Pointers. 2) Reverse the second half of the list. 3) Compare the first half and the reversed second half element by element. 4) (Optional, but good practice) Reverse the second half back to restore the original list.
Notes: Have multiple questions together

Problem Statement: Insert a value into a sorted circular linked list.
Hints: Case Analysis is crucial: 1) The new node is inserted between two nodes where node.val≤insertVal≤node.next.val. 2) The list is empty or has one element. 3) The new node has the minimum/maximum value (requires traversing the list to find the tail/max element).
Notes: Have multiple cases

Problem Statement: Reverse linked list in groups of k.
Hints: (Same as ReverseNodesInKGroup) The key is managing the connection pointers. Use a Dummy Node. Reverse a sublist of size K and connect the previous node to the new head, and the tail of the reversed group to the next un-reversed node.

Problem Statement: Group odd and even positioned nodes together.
Hints: Use Two Pointers (‘odd‘ and ‘even‘ heads) to split the list into two separate lists: one for odd-indexed nodes and one for even-indexed nodes. Finally, connect the tail of the odd list to the head of the even list.

Problem Statement: Implement a snapshot array supporting set(index, value), snap(), and get(index, snapshotId) operations.
Patterns: Custom Data Structure, Persistent Array, Map of Maps
Hints: Create complex datastructure containing Map<Index, TreeMap<SnapId, Value>> to store the value snapshot wise. Another variable `snapId` to get the current snapshot id

Problem Statement: Create an expression that evaluates to a target value.
Hints: Use Backtracking/DFS. Recursively try adding ’+’, ’-’, or ’*’, or appending the current digit to the number formed so far. The recursion terminates when all digits are used.

Problem Statement: Find lexicographically largest string after k swaps.
Hints: Use Backtracking/DFS. The depth of the recursion is k (the max number of swaps). At each step, iterate through all possible pairs of indices (i,j) where string[i]<string[j], swap them, and recurse. Only track the maximum string found.

Problem Statement: Implement a number guessing game.
Hints: Since the API gives a hint (-1,0,1), use Binary Search to quickly converge on the correct number, continuously halving the range [low,high].

Problem Statement: Find minimum amount of money to guarantee a win in guessing game.
Hints: DP State: dp[i][j] is the minimum guaranteed cost to win the game when the range of possible numbers is [i,j]. Recurrence: dp[i][j]=mink=ij​(k+max(dp[i][k−1],dp[k+1][j])). The guess k incurs a cost of k plus the cost of the worst-case subsequent game (max of left or right subproblems).

Problem Statement: Maximize number of events you can attend.
Hints: Greedy Approach: Sort the events by their start day. Iterate through the days from 1 to the maximum end day. On each day, first, add all events that start on that day to a Min-Heap (ordered by their end day). Then, greedily attend the event from the heap that ends earliest.
Notes: Could not understand greedy approach will work here

Problem Statement: Maximize resource access with given constraints.
Hints: Group all access timestamps by resource. For each resource, sort its timestamps and use a sliding window (two pointers) to find the maximum number of accesses within any 5-minute window

Problem Statement: Remove minimum invalid parentheses.
Hints: Use BFS on strings. The states are the strings generated by removing one parenthesis at a time. The first level where a valid parenthesis string is found, all strings at that level are the result (minimizing deletions).

Problem Statement: Generate all permutations of an array.
Hints: Use Backtracking/DFS. The key is to swap the current element with all elements to its right to generate new permutations. Alternatively, use a boolean/set to track used elements.

Problem Statement: Generate all subsets of a set.
Hints: Iterative: Start with an empty set. For each number in the input, create a new subset by adding the current number to all existing subsets. Bit Manipulation: Iterate from 0 to 2N−1. Each number's binary representation corresponds to a subset, where the i-th bit being set means the i-th element is included.
Notes: Iterative approach is hard

Problem Statement: Generate all subsets avoiding duplicates.
Hints: Sort the input array first. Use Backtracking/DFS. The critical step is to skip duplicates: if index>start and nums[index]=nums[index−1], then continue to the next iteration to prevent duplicate subsets.
Notes: Little bit difficult to come up with this

Problem Statement: Count number of solutions to N-Queens problem.
Hints: Bitwise Optimization: Instead of a boolean array/set for columns and diagonals, use three integers (bitmasks): ‘col‘, ‘diag1‘ (row+col), and ‘diag2‘ (row−col). Use bitwise operations to check and mark occupied positions in O(1).
Notes: Bitwise approach required more study

Problem Statement: Generate all permutations of distinct integers.
Hints: Use Backtracking/DFS. At each step, iterate through the elements. If an element is not used, add it to the current permutation, mark it as used, and recurse. Backtrack by unmarking and removing.

Problem Statement: Generate all permutations with possible duplicates.
Hints: Sort the array first. Use Backtracking/DFS. When iterating through the elements, if the current element is the same as the previous one AND the previous one was not used (meaning it was already processed in the loop at the same level), skip the current element to avoid duplicate permutations.

Problem Statement: Generate n-bit Gray code sequence.
Hints: The k-th Gray Code can be calculated directly as k⊕(k/2) or k⊕(k≫1). Alternatively, use a recursive pattern: Gray(n) is Gray(n−1) followed by the reverse of Gray(n−1) with a 1 prepended to each element.
Notes: Bitwise approach required more study

Problem Statement: Check if a string follows a given pattern with full bijection.
Hints: Use Backtracking/DFS. Try every possible split of the string s into a word that maps to the current character in pattern. Use HashMap for pattern_char→word to ensure a one-to-one mapping.
Notes: Not intuitive

Problem Statement: Partition array into two subsets with equal sum.
Hints: This problem reduces to finding if there exists a subset that sums to total_sum/2. DP State: dp[s] is true if a subset with sum s exists. The total sum must be even.

Problem Statement: Find the kth permutation sequence.
Hints: Use the Factorial Number System (or Cantor Expansion) to directly calculate the k-th permutation. At each step, divide k by (N−i)! to find the index of the digit to pick from the remaining pool of numbers.

Problem Statement: Solve the N-Queens puzzle.
Hints: Use Backtracking/DFS. Place one queen per row. For each cell (row,col), check if a queen is already placed in the same column, main diagonal (row−col), or anti-diagonal (row+col).
Notes: Easy approach, faced some issue with diagonal/anti-diagonal

Problem Statement: Find minimum moves for knight to reach destination on chessboard.
Patterns: BFS, Shortest Path in Unweighted Graph
Hints: Since the movement cost is uniform (1 per move), the shortest path is found using Breadth-First Search (BFS). The states are the (row,col) coordinates, and the transitions are the eight possible knight moves.
Notes: Got bit confused with BFS and DP. Easy to implement after that

Problem Statement: Find minimum moves to reach end of snake and ladder board.
Hints: Treat the board as a graph. The shortest path in an unweighted graph is found by BFS. The nodes are the squares. The edges are the dice rolls (1 to 6). Snakes/Ladders are direct edges. Use a ‘visited‘ set to prevent cycles and re-processing.
Notes: Got bit confused with the revisited cells. Easy to implement after that

Problem Statement: Find smallest multiple of a number with only 0s and 1s.
Hints: The state is ‘remainder‘ (modulo a target number N). The initial state is 1(modN). The transitions are multiplying by 10 (appending 0) or multiplying by 10 and adding 1 (appending 1). Use BFS to find the shortest sequence of 0's and 1's that reaches the state 0(modN).

Problem Statement: Find shortest bridge connecting two islands.
Patterns: BFS, Multi-Source BFS, Two BFS Layers
Hints: Two-stage BFS: 1) Use DFS or BFS to find and mark all cells belonging to the first island. 2) Use a Multi-Source BFS starting from all cells of the first island to find the shortest distance to a cell of the second island. The distance (number of 0s traversed) is the number of flips.

Problem Statement: Given a list of bus routes [startPoint, stops ... , endPoint] and back to startPoint. Find minimum number of buses required to travel from a source stop to a destination stop
Hints: This is a Shortest Path problem in a specialized graph. The nodes are the bus stops. The edges represent travelling between stops on a bus route. The challenge is that the "cost" is the number of buses taken. The state in BFS should be (‘stop‘, ‘buses_taken‘) or use a set of unseen bus routes to avoid cycles.

Problem Statement: Make largest island by changing at most one 0 to 1.
Hints: 1) Use DFS/BFS to find all connected components (islands) and calculate the size of each. Store the size in a Map with the component ID. 2) Iterate over all 0 cells. For each 0, check its 4 neighbors. Sum the unique island sizes of its neighbors. The max sum +1 (for the 0 itself) is the answer.

Problem Statement: Count good paths in a tree.
Hints: Use DFS (pre-order traversal). Pass the maximum value seen so far on the path from the root to the current node as an argument to the recursive function. A node is a "good node" if its value is ≥ the max value passed down.

Problem Statement: Maximize meeting invitations where guests are seated in circular table with favorite person sitting next to them
Hints: This is a graph problem with cycles. The max length is from either: 1) A 2-node cycle (A→B→A) plus the longest paths leading into those two nodes. 2) The maximum length of a single cycle of length ≥3.

Problem Statement: Reconstruct itinerary from list of tickets.
Hints: This is an Eulerian Path problem. Use DFS and a Priority Queue (Min-Heap) for the adjacency list to ensure lexicographical order. The result must be built backwards from the recursive calls. The path is formed by visiting all edges, and the stack/list is built on the way back up the recursion.

Problem Statement: Find cells where water can flow to both Pacific and Atlantic oceans.
Hints: The water can flow to the Pacific if there is a path from the cell to the top row or left column. Similarly for the Atlantic (bottom row or right column). Use Two separate DFS/BFS traversals starting from all cells bordering the Pacific and all cells bordering the Atlantic. A cell is the answer if it is reachable by both traversals.

Problem Statement: Find order to finish all courses given prerequisites.
Hints: Use Topological Sort (Kahn's Algorithm with BFS). 1) Calculate the in-degree of all nodes. 2) Initialize a queue with all nodes having an in-degree of 0. 3) While the queue is not empty, dequeue a node, add it to the result, and decrement the in-degree of all its neighbors. If a neighbor's in-degree reaches 0, enqueue it.

Problem Statement: Count valid end states of Tic-Tac-Toe.
Hints: Analyze all possible Tic-Tac-Toe states reachable from an empty board. For a given board, check if it's a valid end state (by checking the number of X's vs O's) and then check for win conditions (row, column, diagonal).

Problem Statement: Perform topological sort on a directed acyclic graph.
Hints: Use either BFS (Kahn's Algorithm) or DFS. BFS is generally preferred for its simplicity in implementation: track in-degrees, and use a queue for 0-in-degree nodes. DFS uses a recursive function and a ‘visited‘ status (unvisited, visiting, visited) to detect cycles.

Problem Statement: Find all recipes that can be created from given supplies.
Hints: This is a Topological Sort problem. Create a graph where an ingredient is a dependency for a recipe. The nodes are recipes and supplies. Calculate the in-degree of each recipe (number of required ingredients). Use BFS starting from the initial ‘supplies‘ to unlock recipes (by reducing their in-degree).

Problem Statement: Minimize maximum cost among components.
Hints: Use Kruskal Algorithm

Problem Statement: Find minimum arrows to burst all balloons.
Hints: This is a Greedy approach similar to interval merging. Sort the balloons by their end coordinate. Iterate through the sorted balloons. The current arrow must be placed at the end coordinate of the last burst balloon to maximize the number of subsequent balloons it can burst.

Problem Statement: Find all shortest transformation sequences from start to end word.
Hints: Two-stage approach: 1) Use BFS to find the shortest distance from ‘beginWord‘ to all other words. This acts as a pruning step. 2) Use DFS (Backtracking) starting from ‘beginWord‘ to find all paths to ‘endWord‘ that are exactly the length found in the BFS.

Problem Statement: Minimize number of transactions to settle debts.
Hints: 1) Calculate the net balance for each person (how much they need to receive/pay). 2) This reduces to a Subset Sum or Partition problem. Use Backtracking/DFS to find the minimum number of transactions to zero out all balances. The state is the list of non-zero balances.

Problem Statement: Find minimum cost path in a grid from top-left to bottom-right.
Hints: DP State: dp[i][j] is the minimum cost to reach cell (i,j) from the top-left corner. Recurrence: dp[i][j]=grid[i][j]+min(dp[i−1][j],dp[i][j−1]).
Notes: Easy to understand after creating graph notes

Problem Statement: Find minEffortRequired to go from top left to bottom right. Effort of path is absolute max difference in height between 2 cells
Find minimum cost to connect all islands.
Hints: This is a standard Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) problem. Use Kruskal's Algorithm with Union-Find to greedily add edges (bridges) in increasing order of weight, as long as they don't form a cycle.

Problem Statement: Find largest distance (no of edges) between any two nodes in a tree.
Hints: The longest path in a tree (the diameter) is found with Two Traversals: 1) Run BFS/DFS from an arbitrary node A to find the farthest node B. 2) Run BFS/DFS from node B to find the farthest node C. The path from B to C is the diameter.

Problem Statement: Find maximal network rank of any two different cities.
Hints: The network rank of two cities (A,B) is degree(A)+degree(B)−is_connected(A,B). Iterate over all pairs of cities (A,B), calculate their rank, and find the maximum.

Problem Statement: Derive order of characters in alien language from sorted words.
Hints: The lexicographical order from the words implies a set of directed edges (charA→charB) where A must come before B. Build a directed graph from these constraints. Then, run Topological Sort (Kahn's BFS or DFS) to find the valid character order.

Problem Statement: Find cheapest flight with at most k stops.
Hints: This is a Shortest Path problem with a constraint on the number of edges (stops). Use a modified BFS or Bellman-Ford algorithm. The BFS state needs to track the number of stops (‘cost‘, ‘stops‘, ‘city‘) to ensure the K stops limit is not exceeded.

Problem Statement: Convert currency using given exchange rates.
Hints: This is a Shortest Path problem in a directed, weighted graph where weights are exchange rates. Use Floyd-Warshall to find all-pairs shortest paths, or a series of DFS/BFS traversals from a source to find the conversion factor.
Notes: Forgot Dijkstra algo

Problem Statement: In a binary matrix, find shortest distance from each 1 to nearest 0
Hints: Use multi source BFS from all the zeroes
Notes: Not able to come up with approach and problem in dijkstra algo

Problem Statement: Determine if you can finish all courses given prerequisites.
Hints: This reduces to cycle detection in a directed graph. A cycle means a course dependency is circular (e.g., A→B→A), making it impossible to finish. Use DFS with three states (unvisited, visiting, visited) to detect a back edge (cycle).

Problem Statement: Find maximum area of island in a grid.
Hints: Use DFS or BFS to explore the connected component of 1's starting from an unvisited 1. Recursively/iteratively count the number of cells in the island. Keep track of the maximum size found.

Problem Statement: Count number of islands in a grid.
Hints: Use DFS or BFS. Iterate over the grid. When a 1 is found, increment the counter and start a traversal (DFS/BFS) from that cell to mark all connected 1's as visited (or change them to 0).

Problem Statement: Capture all regions surrounded by X in a board.
Hints: The only ’O’ regions that cannot be flipped are those connected to the border. Use DFS or BFS starting from all ’O’ cells on the border (first/last row/column) to mark all connected $\text{'O'}$s as safe ($\text{'#'}$). Finally, iterate through the grid: flip ’O’ to ’X’, and flip $\text{'#'}$ back to ’O’.

Problem Statement: Find sum of Fibonacci numbers.
Hints: This uses a property of Fibonacci numbers (Zeckendorf's theorem). Use a greedy approach: start with the largest Fibonacci number ≤k, subtract it from k, and repeat on the remainder.

Problem Statement: Find useful extra edges to add to a graph.
Hints: Run Dijkstra from the start node (S) to all nodes, and again from the end node (E) to all nodes on the reversed graph. Check each extra edge (u,v): if the path dist(S→u)+weight(u,v)+dist(v→E) is less than the current minimum, it's useful.

Problem Statement: Find highest rated item from a list.
Hints: This is a Multi-Stage BFS and sorting problem. 1) Use BFS to find the shortest distance to all reachable items. 2) Store all reachable items as a tuple (‘distance‘, ‘price‘, ‘row‘, ‘col‘) and then sort this list using the custom criteria.

Problem Statement: Find shortest path between two nodes.
Hints: The shortest path in an unweighted graph is found using Breadth-First Search (BFS). The distance of a node is its ‘level‘ in the BFS tree.

Problem Statement: Find length of shortest transformation sequence from start to end word.
Hints: Use BFS. The state is the word. The transitions are one-letter changes. Optimization: Use Bidirectional BFS. Start two BFS traversals, one from ‘beginWord‘ and one from ‘endWord‘. When the two search frontiers meet, a shortest path has been found. This significantly reduces the search space.
Notes: Bidirectional BFS and pattern using is difficult to think

Problem Statement: Simulate Minesweeper game reveal.
Hints: This is a DFS/BFS problem. If a click reveals a bomb (’M’), change it to ’X’. If it's an empty square, count the number of adjacent bombs. If count>0, change the cell to count (e.g., '1', '2'). If count=0, change it to ’B’ and recursively/iteratively reveal all its unrevealed neighbors.

Problem Statement: given an array of prerequisite pairs and a list of query pairs.For each query [u, v], determine whether u is a prerequisite of v (directly or indirectly).
Hints: Use the Floyd-Warshall algorithm to calculate all-pairs reachability: reachable[i][j]=reachable[i][j]∨(reachable[i][k]∧reachable[k][j]).

Problem Statement: Find minimum cost to achieve a target sum.
Hints: DP State: dp[i] is the minimum cost to reach the i-th step. Recurrence: dp[i]=cost[i]+min(dp[i−1],dp[i−2]) (or similar for the specific problem constraints).

Problem Statement: Search for a word in a 2D board.
Hints: Use Backtracking/DFS. Start a DFS from every cell in the grid. The DFS explores the 4 directions. The recursive call only proceeds if the next letter in the word matches the cell's character. Mark the current cell as visited for the duration of the recursive path.

Problem Statement: Given a 2D board and a list of words from the dictionary, find all words that can be formed by sequentially adjacent letters from board
Hints: Optimization: Build a Trie from the list of words. Use DFS (Backtracking) on the grid. At each cell, check if the path formed so far is a prefix in the Trie. If a complete word is found, add it to the result and continue searching. This avoids checking a word that doesn't exist.

Problem Statement: Evaluate division equations using given variable relationships.
Hints: Treat the problem as a graph where nodes are variables and edge weights are the ratio. A/B=2.0 is an edge A→B with weight 2.0 and B→A with weight 1/2.0. Use DFS or BFS to find the path between the query variables. The result is the product of the edge weights along the path.

Problem Statement: Find maximum in each sliding window of size k.
Hints: Use a Monotonic Decreasing Deque. The deque stores the indices of elements. The front of the deque is always the index of the maximum element in the current window. When adding a new element, remove all smaller elements from the back of the deque. Before adding the new element, remove the index at the front if it's outside the window.

Problem Statement: Find minimum time to complete tasks with cooling intervals.
Hints: This is a Greedy approach based on frequency. The minimum time is determined by the most frequent task (max_freq). The time is at least (max_freq−1)×(n+1)+count(max_freq). The total time is the max of this calculated time and the total number of tasks.

Problem Statement: Find all pairs of words that form palindromes when concatenated.
Hints: Optimization: Use a Trie to store the reverse of all words. For each word in the original list, search for it in the reverse Trie. A pair (word1,word2) forms a palindrome if: 1) word2 is the reverse of word1. 2) word1 is prefix+palindrome and word2 is reverse(prefix). 3) word2 is palindrome+suffix and word1 is reverse(suffix).

Problem Statement: Find most visited website pattern.
Patterns: Hash Map, Time Series Analysis, Frequency
Hints: Group the website visits by user. For each user, extract all contiguous sequences of three website visits (trip1,trip2,trip3) in chronological order. Use a HashMap to count the frequency of each unique 3-sequence.

Problem Statement: Partition string into maximum parts where each letter appears in at most one part.
Hints: Greedy Approach: The key is to find the last occurrence of every character in the string. Iterate through the string, maintaining the maximum last index ‘max_end‘ of all characters seen so far in the current partition. When the current index i equals ‘max_end‘, a valid partition is found.

Problem Statement: Design data structure supporting inc, dec, getMaxKey, getMinKey in O(1).
Patterns: Data Structure Design, Doubly Linked List, Hash Map
Hints: This requires a complex structure for O(1) operations. Use a HashMap to map key→‘node‘ and a Doubly Linked List where each node represents a frequency level and contains a Set of all keys with that frequency. ‘inc‘ moves a key from one Set to the next frequency node's Set.

Problem Statement: Find if there exists a subarray with sum zero.
Hints: Use a HashMap to store the prefix sum and its index. If current_sum is seen again, the subarray between the two indices sums to 0. The length of the longest subarray is current_index−old_index.

Problem Statement: Reconstruct digits from jumbled string of digit words. "owoztneoer" -> "012"
Hints: This uses a frequency map of characters. The key is that certain digits have unique characters (e.g., 'z' for zero, 'w' for two, 'u' for four, 'x' for six, 'g' for eight). Solve for these first, then use their counts to find the remaining digits.
Notes: Unintuitive approach. Easy code after looking at it

Problem Statement: Find duplicate files in a file system.
Hints: Use a HashMap where the key is the file content and the value is a list of all file paths containing that content. Iterate through the paths, extract the content and file name, and populate the map.

Problem Statement: Maximize number of events you can attend.
Hints: (Same as previous MaxEvents entry). Sort by start day. Iterate through days. Use a Min-Heap to store available events, ordered by end day. Greedily attend the event that ends earliest.
Notes: Easy after clicking approach

Problem Statement: Maximize value gained from attending event [startDay, endDay, value]. You can attend at max k events
Hints: DP State: dp[d][k] is the maximum value that can be collected by attending at most k events up to day d. Recurrence: dp[d][k]=max(dp[d−1][k],dp[start−1][k−1]+value). Sort events by end day.

Problem Statement: Find k pairs with smallest sums combination from two sorted arrays.
Hints: This is similar to a K-way merge. Use a Min-Heap to store (‘sum‘, ‘index1‘, ‘index2‘). Initialize the heap with (nums1[i]+nums2[0]) for all i. After extracting a pair, add the next pair from the second array to the heap: (nums1[i]+nums2[j+1]).

Problem Statement: Find kth smallest element in a matrix with sorted rows and columns.
Patterns: Binary Search on Answer, Min-Heap (K-way Merge)
Hints: Insert (value, i, j) in min heap. Poll it from minHeap and then insert values below and right of that position

Problem Statement: Find kth largest element in an array.
Hints: The most efficient solution is Quickselect (average O(N)), which is a modification of Quicksort's partition. Alternatively, use a Min-Heap of size K (insert/delete is O(logK), total O(NlogK)).

Problem Statement: Design class to find kth largest element in a stream.
Hints: Use a Min-Heap of size K. When a new element arrives, push it onto the heap. If the heap size exceeds K, pop the minimum element (the root). The root of the final size-K heap is the K-th largest element.

Problem Statement: Merge k sorted linked lists.
Hints: This is a standard K-way merge problem. Use a Min-Heap to store the head node of each of the K lists. Continuously extract the minimum node, add it to the result, and insert the next node from that same list into the heap.

Problem Statement: Distribute candies to children so that each child get atleast one candy and children with higher rating get more candy then immediate neighbor
Hints: Use a Greedy approach with two passes. 1) Left-to-Right: Ensure that the right child with a higher rating gets more candy than the left child. 2) Right-to-Left: Ensure that the left child with a higher rating gets more candy than the right child. The final candy count for a child is the maximum of the two passes.
Notes: Not able to come up with approach. Understood after

Problem Statement: GIven a string representing filled/empty seats in a line. Find minimum moves to aggregate all seats together. A move means moving a person one position left or right to an empty seat.
Hints: The minimum cost to move points to a single point is achieved by moving them to the median point. The cost calculation can be optimized using Prefix Sum to quickly find the total movement cost for any given median point.

Problem Statement: Find majority element appearing more than n/2 times.
Hints: Boyer-Moore Voting Algorithm: Iterate through the array. Maintain a ‘count‘ and a ‘candidate‘. If count=0, set ‘candidate‘ to the current element. If element=‘candidate‘, increment count; otherwise, decrement count. The final ‘candidate‘ is the majority element.
Notes: Different appraoch. Easy implementation

Problem Statement: Shuffle an array randomly.
Hints: Implement the Fisher-Yates (Knuth) Shuffle algorithm. Iterate from the last element to the first. At each index i, swap the element nums[i] with a randomly chosen element from the prefix nums[0…i].

Problem Statement: Find kth factor of a number.
Hints: Iterate from 1 up to N​. If i divides N, then i and N/i are factors. Collect all factors and sort them, or keep track of two pointers for i and N/i to avoid sorting.

Problem Statement: Divide two integers without using multiplication, division, or mod operators.
Hints: The core idea is to find Q such that D×Q≤dividend<D×(Q+1). Use Binary Search to find Q. The multiplication D×Q is done efficiently using left bit shifts (e.g., D×2k is D≪k) to find the largest 2k multiple of the divisor that is ≤ the remainder.
Notes: DIdn't understand the bit manipulations part

Problem Statement: Find integer square root of a number.
Hints: Use Binary Search on the range [1,x] (or [1,x/2]). For a mid-value M, check if M×M is equal to, less than, or greater than x. Adjust the search range accordingly.
Notes: Difficult to implement

Problem Statement: Find all prime numbers up to n using Sieve of Eratosthenes.
Hints: The Sieve of Eratosthenes is an algorithm for finding all prime numbers up to a given limit N. Start with a list of all integers. Mark multiples of the first prime (2) as composite, then the multiples of the next unmarked number (3), and so on.

Problem Statement: Calculate x raised to power n.
Hints: For each iteration, if the exponent is odd, multiply the result by the current base; then square the base and halve the exponent. Handle negative exponents by taking the reciprocal of the base upfront. Convert n to long to safely handle Integer.MIN_VALUE edge case.

Problem Statement: Perform division of two numbers.
Hints: (Same as IntegerDivisionWithoutOperators) The key is using bit shifts (≪) to efficiently multiply the divisor by powers of 2 and subtract the largest possible multiple from the dividend.

Problem Statement: Find next palindromic time.
Patterns: String Manipulation, Time/Clock Arithmetic, Palindrome Check
Hints: Iterate through the hours and minutes (from the given time) to find the next time that is a palindrome when represented as a 4-digit string (e.g., '10:01', '02:20').

Problem Statement: Validate if a string is a valid number.
Hints: Implement a Finite State Machine (FSM). Define states for the start, sign, integer part, decimal point, fractional part, exponent 'e', exponent sign, and exponent integer. Transition between states based on the current character.
Notes: Not useful for interviews

Problem Statement: Parse CSV data.
Hints: Use a State Machine to handle the three states: 1) Unquoted field (comma is a delimiter). 2) Quoted field (comma is not a delimiter, double quotes are escaped). 3) Quote ending (check for a second quote or a comma).
Notes: Handling the enclosed quote is edge case

Problem Statement: Find longest palindromic substring.
Patterns: Dynamic Programming, Two Pointers (Expand from Center)
Hints: DP State: dp[i][j] is true if the substring s[i…j] is a palindrome. Recurrence: dp[i][j]=s[i]=s[j]∧dp[i+1][j−1]. Optimization (Expand from Center): Iterate through all possible centers (i for odd length, i,i+1 for even length) and expand outwards as long as the characters match.
Notes: Not intuitive appraoch, Easy to implement after looking at code

Problem Statement: Convert Roman numeral to integer.
Hints: The main rule is additive, but the subtractive rule (e.g., ’IV’=4,’IX’=9) is the tricky part. Iterate through the string, and if the current symbol's value is less than the next symbol's value, subtract the current value; otherwise, add it.
Notes: Non intuitive approach, easy to implement after looking at code

Problem Statement: Find first occurrence of substring in a string.
Hints: The naive approach is O(N×M). The optimized approach uses the KMP (Knuth-Morris-Pratt) algorithm, which uses a pre-calculated LPS (Longest Proper Prefix which is also a Suffix) array to avoid unnecessary re-matching.

Problem Statement: Construct longest happy string with given character counts.
Hints: This is a Greedy problem. Use a Max-Heap to store the counts of the characters. At each step, greedily choose the most frequent character, but only if it doesn't violate the three-in-a-row constraint. If the most frequent character would violate the constraint, use the second most frequent one once.

Problem Statement: Generate nth term of count-and-say sequence.
Hints: This is a recursive string generation. ‘CountAndSay‘(n) is generated by "reading aloud" the string from ‘CountAndSay‘(n−1). The key is iterating through the previous string and counting contiguous identical characters.

Problem Statement: Reverse a string.
Hints: Use Two Pointers (‘left‘ and ‘right‘) starting at the beginning and end of the string. In a loop, swap the characters at the two pointers and move them towards the center until they cross.

Problem Statement: Check if one string is a rotation of another.
Hints: A string B is a rotation of A if B is a substring of A+A and length(A)=length(B). The check is len(A)=len(B)∧(B in A+A).

Problem Statement: Perform find and replace operations on a string.
Hints: To avoid indices shifting after a replacement, perform the replacements in reverse order (from the end of the string to the beginning). Sort the indices array in descending order.
Notes: Just need to remember reverse replacement

Problem Statement: Reorder log files with letter-logs before digit-logs.
Hints: Implement a custom Comparator for the list of logs. The rules are: 1) Letter logs come before digit logs. 2) Letter logs are sorted lexicographically by content, then by identifier. 3) Digit logs remain in their original order.

Problem Statement: Find minimum insertions to make string a palindrome.
Hints: The minimum number of insertions to make a string a palindrome is equal to the length of the string minus the Length of the Longest Palindromic Subsequence (LPS). LPS is equivalent to the Longest Common Subsequence (LCS) of the original string and its reverse.

Problem Statement: Convert string to integer (atoi).
Hints: Implement a state machine or use careful parsing to handle the sequence: whitespace → sign → digits → non-digit. The main difficulty is handling Integer Overflow (clamping the result to INT_MAX or INT_MIN).

Problem Statement: Construct repeat limited string.
Hints: Use a Max-Heap or a reverse-ordered Frequency Map to greedily pick the largest available character. The constraint is that a character can be repeated at most k times. If the largest character ‘A‘ is at its limit, insert a single instance of the next largest character ‘B‘ before resuming with ‘A‘.

Problem Statement: Find longest chunked palindrome decomposition.
Hints: Use Two Pointers (‘left‘ and ‘right‘) to check for equal prefix and suffix. ‘left‘ starts at 0, ‘right‘ starts at length−1. At each step, increase the prefix length L and check if text[0…L−1]=text[length−L…length−1].

Problem Statement: Convert integer to Roman numeral.
Hints: Use a map of Roman values and their symbols, sorted in descending order (e.g., 1000→’M’). Iterate through the map and greedily subtract the largest possible value from N, appending the corresponding symbol to the result.

Problem Statement: Rearrange string so no two adjacent characters are the same.
Hints: This is a Greedy approach based on frequency. The most frequent character cannot have a count greater than ⌈length/2⌉. Use a Max-Heap to store character counts. Greedily pick the two most frequent characters and append them to the result one by one.

Problem Statement: Convert string to zigzag pattern.
Patterns: String Manipulation, Pattern Recognition, Row Tracking
Hints: The pattern is based on the period of the zigzag: 2×(numRows−1). For each row i, the characters occur at indices k⋅period+i and k⋅period+(period−i).

Problem Statement: Find longest substring with at least k repeating characters.
Hints: This uses a Divide and Conquer approach with frequency analysis. If a character's frequency is less than K, it cannot be part of the final answer. Use that character as a delimiter to split the string into smaller substrings and recursively solve the problem on those substrings.

Problem Statement: Justify text to fit within given width.
Hints: Greedy: Fit as many words as possible on the current line. The main difficulty is the even distribution of spaces. Calculate total_spaces. Distribute total_spaces/(num_gaps) spaces to each gap, and distribute the remainder total_spacesmod(num_gaps) as one extra space to the first few gaps. The last line is left-justified.
Notes: Operationally difficult

Problem Statement: Find length of longest substring without repeating characters.
Hints: Use a Sliding Window with two pointers (L,R). Use a HashSet to track the characters currently in the window. If s[R] is already in the set, move L to 1 position after the first occurrence of s[R], and update the set.

Problem Statement: Arrange numbers to form largest number.
Hints: The problem requires a custom sorting comparator. For two numbers A and B, they should be sorted such that the number formed by concatenating them, ‘str‘(A)+‘str‘(B), is greater than ‘str‘(B)+‘str‘(A).
Notes: Confusing comparator

Problem Statement: Find all anagrams of a pattern in a string.
Hints: Use a Sliding Window of size len(p). Use a Frequency Map (or array of size 26) for both the target p and the current window of s. The window is an anagram if the two frequency maps are equal. Optimization: Use a single ‘count‘ variable to track the number of characters in the window whose frequency is still missing from the target.
Notes: Didn't get logic of shrinking the window

Problem Statement: Count binary strings without consecutive 1s.
Hints: DP/Combinatorics: The number of binary strings of length L with no consecutive 1's is related to Fibonacci numbers. Fib(L)=count(ends in 0)+count(ends in 1). The problem is solved by counting the total and subtracting the count of numbers >N that also satisfy the non-consecutive 1's rule.
Notes: Very hard to understand the subtraction logic

Problem Statement: Maximize number of good people based on statements.
Hints: The number of people N is small (≤15). This suggests a 2N solution. Use Backtracking or Brute Force with a bitmask to represent all 2N possible subsets of "good people." For each subset, check if it's a consistent assignment (i.e., all statements made by the "good people" are true).
Notes: Bit manipulation approach is impossible. Backtracking is good

Problem Statement: Find maximum XOR of two numbers in an array.
Hints: To maximize XOR, traverse a Binary Trie and try to always choose the opposite bit (0 XOR 1, 1 XOR 0) at the most significant positions first.

Problem Statement: Find pair with maximum bitwise AND.
Hints: The key is to check the bits from the Most Significant Bit (MSB) down. Find the largest possible number $x$ such that at least $k$ elements in the array satisfy element & x == x.

Problem Statement: Find minimum coins needed to make a target amount.
Hints: DP State: dp[i] is the minimum number of coins to make an amount i. Recurrence: dp[i]=minc∈coins​(dp[i−c])+1.

Problem Statement: Implement regular expression matching with . and *.
Hints: The state for the DP is dp[i][j], which is true if s[i:] matches p[j:]. Handle the * case by considering two options: matching zero of the preceding element or matching one (or more) and then advancing s.

Problem Statement: Find minimum operations to reach target using broken calculator.
Hints: Start from the target and work backwards to startValue. If target is even, divide by 2. If target is odd, you must add 1 to make it even, as multiplying by 2 from a smaller number is more costly.

Problem Statement: Find longest common substring between two strings.
Hints: DP State: dp[i][j] is the length of the longest common suffix of text1[0…i] and text2[0…j]. Recurrence: If text1[i]=text2[j], then dp[i][j]=dp[i−1][j−1]+1; otherwise, dp[i][j]=0. The answer is max(dp[i][j]) across the whole table.

Problem Statement: Given initial fuel, gas stations with distance and fuel, destination. Find minimum refueling stops to reach destination.
Hints: Greedy Approach: At each fuel station, if the current fuel is not enough to reach the next station, use a Max-Heap to refuel from the stations passed so far with the maximum available gas.
Notes: Still didn't get DP approach even though its not optimal

Problem Statement: Given an array of coins. Maximize coins collected by player 1 in a line game.
Hints: DP State: dp[i][j] is the maximum score the current player can get from the coins coins[i…j]. Recurrence: The current player chooses either coins[i] or coins[j]. The remaining score for the opponent must be minimized: dp[i][j]=max(coins[i]+min(dp[i+2][j],dp[i+1][j−1]),coins[j]+min(dp[i+1][j−1],dp[i][j−2])).
Notes: Hard intuition, easy for implementation

Problem Statement: Maximize profit from overlapping job scheduling where each job has some profit
Patterns: Dynamic Programming, Binary Search, Interval Scheduling
Hints: DP State: dp[i] is the maximum profit by considering jobs up to job[i]. Sort jobs by end time. Recurrence: dp[i]=max(dp[i−1],profit[i]+dp[prev_non_overlapping_job]). The index of ‘prev_non_overlapping_job‘ is found using Binary Search on the sorted end times.
Notes: Could come up with the appraoch, binary search to find prev non overlapping is bit challenging

Problem Statement: Count distinct subsequences of one string in another.
Hints: DP State: dp[i][j] is the number of distinct subsequences of text1[0…i−1] that equal text2[0…j−1]. Recurrence: If text1[i−1]=text2[j−1], then dp[i][j]=dp[i−1][j−1]+dp[i−1][j]. If text1[i−1]=text2[j−1], then dp[i][j]=dp[i−1][j].

Problem Statement: Find minimum path sum from top to bottom of triangle.
Hints: To find the minimum path sum, start from the bottom row and move upwards. The minimum path sum at (i, j) is triangle[i][j] + min(dp[i+1][j], dp[i+1][j+1]). You can optimize space by only storing one row's results.

Problem Statement: Maximize coins by bursting balloons.
Hints: DP State: dp[i][j] is the maximum coins from bursting all balloons in the range open interval (i,j). The key is to iterate on the balloon burst last in the range, say k. dp[i][j]=maxi<k<j​(dp[i][k]+dp[k][j]+coins[i]×coins[k]×coins[j]).
Notes: Could not come up with approach. This has DP with based on left and right index, not intuitive like knapsack

Problem Statement: Implement wildcard pattern matching with ? and *.
Hints: DP State: dp[i][j] is true if the prefix s[0…i−1] matches p[0…j−1]. The recurrence is complex, mainly due to the ’*’. Optimization (Greedy/Two Pointers): Use a ‘star_match‘ pointer to handle the ’*’, backtracking when the current match fails.

Problem Statement: Check if s3 is formed by interleaving s1 and s2.
Hints: DP State: dp[i][j] is true if s3[0…i+j−1] is an interleaving of s1[0…i−1] and s2[0…j−1]. Recurrence: dp[i][j]=(dp[i−1][j]∧s1[i−1]=s3[i+j−1])∨(dp[i][j−1]∧s2[j−1]=s3[i+j−1]).
Notes: Bit tricky appraoch but easy to code after understanding the appraoch

Problem Statement: Check if string can be segmented into dictionary words.
Hints: DP State: dp[i] is true if the prefix s[0…i−1] can be segmented. Recurrence: dp[i]=⋁j<i​(dp[j]∧s[j…i−1]∈dictionary).

Problem Statement: Given a string s and a dictionary wordDict, return all possible ways to break s into a sequence of dictionary words. Each word must appear in the dictionary, and the same word can be reused multiple times.
Hints: Use recursion with memoization (top-down DP). At each index, try all possible word endings—if a substring matches a dictionary word, recursively solve for the remaining string. Memoize results by start index to avoid recomputation. Build sentences by concatenating valid prefixes with recursive suffix results, achieving O(N²) time with pruning.

Problem Statement: Maximize profit with stock transactions and cooldown.
Hints: Define three states for each day: buy (max profit if you hold a stock), sell (max profit if you sell today), and cooldown (max profit if you did nothing today/are in cooldown).

Problem Statement: Find maximum profit from buying and selling stock once.
Hints: This is the basic Buy/Sell Stock I problem. Find the min price to buy and maximize sell_price−min_buy.

Problem Statement: Maximize profit with transaction fee.
Hints: DP State: ‘hold‘[i] (max profit if holding a stock on day i) and ‘free‘[i] (max profit if not holding a stock on day i). Recurrence: ‘hold‘[i]=max(‘hold‘[i−1],‘free‘[i−1]−price[i]). ‘free‘[i]=max(‘free‘[i−1],‘hold‘[i−1]+price[i]−fee).

Problem Statement: Maximize profit with at most two transactions.
Hints: DP State: ‘buy1‘, ‘sell1‘, ‘buy2‘, ‘sell2‘ represent the max profit after the 1st buy, 1st sell, 2nd buy, and 2nd sell, respectively. The maximum profit is constrained by K=2 transactions.

Problem Statement: Maximize profit with at most k transactions.
Hints: Use a 2D DP array, dp[k][i], representing the max profit with at most k transactions up to day i. Handle the edge case where $k$ is large enough to allow unlimited transactions (similar to 'Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock II').

Problem Statement: Maximize brain power from solving questions.
Hints: DP State: dp[i] is the maximum points obtainable by considering questions from index i to the end. Recurrence: dp[i]=max(points[i]+dp[i+brainpower[i]+1],dp[i+1]).
Notes: Got bit confused on the intution but easy implementation

Problem Statement: Count submatrices with all ones.
Hints: For each cell (r, c), calculate the height $h[r][c]$ of consecutive ones extending up from that cell. The problem then reduces to finding the sum of areas of all histograms formed by these heights in each row.

Problem Statement: Calculate champagne in a glass after pouring.
Hints: The amount of fluid that flows to glass[i][j] is (fluid−1.0)/2 from glass[i−1][j−1] and (fluid−1.0)/2 from glass[i−1][j]. DP State: dp[i][j] is the amount of fluid that pours into glass (i,j).
Notes: Easy to come up with approach and solution

Problem Statement: Find minimum path sum in a grid.
Hints: DP State: dp[i][j] is the minimum path sum to reach cell (i,j). Recurrence: dp[i][j]=grid[i][j]+min(dp[i−1][j],dp[i][j−1]).

Problem Statement: Count unique characters in all substrings.
Hints: The key is to calculate the contribution of each character S[i] to the total count. S[i] contributes 1 to the unique count of a substring S[L…R] if it's the only instance of that character in the substring. Use two arrays to store the index of the previous and next occurrence of the same character.

Problem Statement: Find minimum cost to merge stone piles if we can merge k piles at a time. Cost of merging is equal to the value of piles
Patterns: Dynamic Programming, Range DP, Prefix Sum
Hints: DP State: dp[i][j] is the minimum cost to merge the stones from index i to j into a single pile. Recurrence: dp[i][j]=mink=ij−1​(dp[i][k]+dp[k+1][j])+sum(stones[i…j]). Requires the interval to be mergeable (size mod(K−1)=1). Use Prefix Sum for O(1) sum calculation.

Problem Statement: Find maximum length of pair chain.
Hints: Greedy Approach: Sort the pairs based on their end value (the second element). Iterate through the sorted pairs and greedily select the next pair whose start value is strictly greater than the end value of the previously selected pair.

Problem Statement: Check if array can be partitioned into equal sum subsets.
Hints: This is a standard Subset Sum problem. DP State: dp[i][s] is true if a sum s can be formed using the first i items. Recurrence: dp[i][s]=dp[i−1][s]∨dp[i−1][s−item].
Notes: Easy with recursive aproach, difficulty in understanding the iterative appraoch

Problem Statement: Solve 0/1 knapsack problem.

Notes: Easy with recursive aproach, difficult last row condition in the iterative approach

Find shortest subarray with sum at least k.

Problem Statement: Count subsets with given difference.
Hints: The problem reduces to finding the number of subsets with a specific sum S. Let S1 and S2 be the two subset sums. S1−S2=diff and S1+S2=total_sum. Solving for S1 gives S1=(total_sum+diff)/2. This requires total_sum+diff to be non-negative and even.
Notes: Easy after doing CountSubsetWithGivenSum

Problem Statement: Find all concatenated words in a list.
Hints: 1) Build a Trie from all the words for O(1) prefix look-up. 2) Use Dynamic Programming/Memoization to check if a word can be segmented into a sequence of other words from the dictionary.

Problem Statement: Count ways to tile a board with dominoes.
Hints: DP State: dp[i] is the number of ways to tile a 2×i board. Recurrence: dp[i]=dp[i−1]+dp[i−2] (equivalent to Fibonacci sequence).

Problem Statement: Count ways to tile 2xN board with dominoes.
Hints: This is a variation of the Tiling problem, often requiring Matrix Exponentiation to solve for large N in O(logN) time. The recurrence matrix is difficult to derive.

Problem Statement: Count ways to tile board with dominoes and trominos.

The recurrence involves dp[i] depending on dp[i−1], dp[i−2], and dp_partial[i−1].

Problem Statement: Check if frog can cross river if it can take (k-1), k or (k+1) jump from prev

Problem Statement: Find minimum flips for non-negative prefix sum.

Problem Statement: Check if one string is a scrambled version of another.

Problem Statement: Maximize points by deleting numbers and earning points.

Problem Statement: Determine winner of stone game.
Hints: DP State: dp[i][j] is the maximum difference in score the current player can achieve over the opponent from the stones stones[i…j]. Recurrence: dp[i][j]=max(stones[i]−dp[i+1][j],stones[j]−dp[i][j−1]). The key insight is that stones[i]−dp[i+1][j] is the score you get minus the maximum score the opponent can get from the remaining stones.
Notes: Easy recursive approach, hard iterative

Problem Statement: Find longest common subsequence between two strings.

Problem Statement: Find queens that can attack the king.
Hints: For each of the eight directions from the King's position, iterate outwards until either a Queen is found or the board edge is reached. A queen only attacks if it is the first piece encountered in that direction.

Problem Statement: Count ways to make amount using given coins.

Problem Statement: Maximize profit from cutting rod.

Problem Statement: Design a call counter.
Hints: Use a Queue (or a Deque) to store the timestamps of the calls. When a new call arrives, first dequeue all timestamps that are older than ‘t‘ seconds ago. The size of the remaining queue is the number of recent calls.

Problem Statement: Find length of longest valid parentheses substring.
Hints: DP State: dp[i] is the length of the longest valid parenthesis substring ending at index i. Recurrence: If s[i]=’)’ and s[i−1]=’(’, then dp[i]=dp[i−2]+2. If s[i]=’)’ and s[i−1]=’)’ and the matching open parenthesis is at i−dp[i−1]−1, then dp[i]=dp[i−1]+dp[i−dp[i−1]−2]+2.

Problem Statement: Count ways to decode a digit string.
Hints: DP State: dp[i] is the number of ways to decode the prefix s[0…i−1]. Recurrence: dp[i] depends on: 1) the single digit s[i−1] (if it's not '0'), and 2) the two digits s[i−2…i−1] (if it forms a number between 10 and 26).

Problem Statement: Check if parentheses are valid.

Problem Statement: Implement queue using stack.

Problem Statement: Remove nodes from linked list based on condition.
Hints: Optimization: Use a Stack to process the list. Iterate through the list, and for each node, pop all nodes from the stack that have a smaller value than the current node. The remaining nodes in the stack (in reverse order) form the resulting list.

Problem Statement: Remove nth node from end of linked list.
Hints: Use a Dummy Node. Use Two Pointers (‘fast‘ and ‘slow‘) separated by N nodes. Move ‘fast‘ N steps ahead, then move both pointers together until ‘fast‘ reaches the end. ‘slow‘ will be pointing to the node before the one to be removed.

Problem Statement: Merge two sorted linked lists.

Problem Statement: Design a call counter.
Hints: (Same as previous CallCounter entry). Use a Queue to store timestamps and remove those outside the 3000ms window.

Problem Statement: Detect cycle in a linked list.
Hints: Floyd's Cycle-Finding Algorithm (Tortoise and Hare): Use Two Pointers (‘slow‘ moves by 1, ‘fast‘ moves by 2). If the list is cyclic, they will eventually meet.

Problem Statement: Check if two strings are anagrams.
Hints: Use a Frequency Map (or array of size 26) to count the characters in the first string. Then, iterate through the second string, decrementing the count. Anagrams are valid if all counts are 0 at the end.

Problem Statement: Find first unique character in a string.
Hints: Use a Frequency Map (or array of size 26) in the first pass to count all character occurrences. In the second pass, iterate through the string and return the index of the first character whose count is 1.

Problem Statement: Simulate vacuum cleaner robot.

Problem Statement: Check if a string is a palindrome.

Problem Statement: Check if string can be palindrome after deleting at most one character.

Problem Statement: Find longest common prefix among strings.

Problem Statement: Add two binary strings.

Problem Statement: Print tree sideways.

Problem Statement: Find the kth permutation sequence.
Hints: Use factorial-based indexing to directly compute the kth permutation without generating all previous ones. At each position, divide k by (n-1)! to determine which remaining number to pick, then update k using modulo and reduce the factorial. This avoids O(n!) backtracking by mathematically determining each digit in O(n) time, achieving O(n²) total complexity due to list removals.
